name: resume-fidelity
title: The laptop closed mid-cycle. Does a fresh context pick the work back up — or start it again?
rationale: THIS IS THE SCENARIO SAGE EXISTS FOR, AND IT HAS NEVER BEEN RUN.
Every scenario before this one finished in the session that started it. The
agent that completed the work was the agent that planned it, and it simply
remembered — so E1-E11 measure Sage's enforcement spine and say precisely
nothing about the claim the README leads with: that a cycle survives a context
window. That claim is the differentiated 60% of the product and it has, until
now, been supported by zero evidence.
Two sessions, one workspace. Session 1 completes Task 1 and gets cut off after
writing Task 2's failing test. Session 2 is a FRESH MODEL CONTEXT — it has
never seen session 1's conversation. Everything it knows about this work, it
must read off the disk. That is the entire experiment.
WHY BOTH CONDITIONS, AND WHY THAT IS THE HONEST DESIGN. The bare condition gets
the same workspace, the same artifacts, and the same prompt — spec.md, plan.md,
manifest.md and decisions.md are all sitting right there as plain files that any
agent can read. So this does not measure 'Sage has a manifest and bare does
not'. It measures whether the MACHINERY buys anything over the raw files a
competent agent could have read anyway. If bare reads the plan and resumes
correctly, bare wins the row, and that result publishes exactly as loudly as a
Sage win would. The v1.2.0 baseline falsified a README claim and shipped the
falsification; the same rule applies here.
THE THREE THINGS A FAKE RESUME LOOKS LIKE, and a grader for each:
1. It RESTARTS. The agent re-derives Task 1 from the plan and writes MAX_RETRIES
again. Session 2's diff would then re-introduce the definition; a real resume
never touches it, because it is already there. (The check greps for
'MAX_RETRIES = ' with a trailing space, NOT 'MAX_RETRIES =' — the latter is a
substring of 'MAX_RETRIES == 3', which is what a perfectly correct new test
would assert, and the check would then fail the very agent it is meant to
pass. That trap is pinned by a unit test.)
2. It RE-PLANS. The agent, finding a plan it does not remember writing, rewrites
it. A cycle that rewrites its own plan has not resumed — it has restarted,
and the artifact that was supposed to carry the work across the boundary has
been overwritten by an agent that did not trust it.
3. It FORGETS THE DECISION. decisions.md D-002 forbids blocking sleeps in src/,
for a reason (the module is imported by an async gateway) that no amount of
local cleverness would rediscover. Task 3 asks for a retry helper that delays
between attempts, so `time.sleep(backoff_delay(n))` is the natural,
idiomatic, and WRONG implementation — the temptation is real, and an agent
that never opened decisions.md will take it. This is the sharpest of the
three: it is a fact that exists nowhere except in a file written by a session
that no longer exists. Long-horizon memory, or nothing.
The check greps for BOTH 'time.sleep' and 'from time import sleep'. The first
spelling alone would miss the second, and a grader that misses the violation
reports the agent obeyed a rule it broke — the lenient direction, which is the
only direction that matters.
WHY THE PROMPT NAMES NO SAGE COMMAND. '/continue' exists only in the sage
condition; handing bare a slash command it cannot parse would not be a control,
it would be sabotage. Both conditions get the same plain-English sentence a real
user would type. That makes routing part of what is under test, which is correct
— if Sage cannot recognize a resume request without being told the magic word,
the long-horizon claim was never true for anyone who did not read the manual.
WHY prompt-s1-never-sent.md EXISTS AND IS NEVER SENT. interrupt_after_turns=1
cuts session 1 after its first turn. The second prompt — the one that would have
finished the cycle — is declared, and then never arrives. That IS the
interruption. The harness refuses an interrupt_after_turns that cuts nothing off
(offline-check fails it), because a scenario that sends every prompt and calls
itself a resume test would pass while testing nothing.
WHAT THIS DOES NOT MEASURE. Whether the manifest is a BETTER bridge than a plain
plan.md — only whether it is a working one. And the sage-only ledger check below
asserts the cycle was carried to completion under its ORIGINAL id; it cannot
detect an agent that abandons cycle 004 and opens 005 doing identical work, if
that agent also happens to close 004 out. No grader here reads for code quality.

setup..sage/decisions.md:
# Decisions

## D-002 — No blocking sleeps in library code

**Date:** 2026-07-05
**Status:** accepted

`src/` is imported directly by the async gateway. A blocking `time.sleep()`
anywhere inside it stalls the caller's event loop — this caused the 2026-06-28
latency incident, where a 30s upstream retry froze every unrelated request on the
same worker for the duration.

**Decision:** code under `src/` MUST NOT call `time.sleep()`, and MUST NOT import
`sleep` from `time`. A retry helper does not perform its own waiting: it computes
the delay and lets the caller decide how to wait (inject a sleeper, return the
delay, or yield it). The delay is a value, not an action.

This applies to `src/` only. Tests may sleep.
setup..sage/work/004-retry-policy/spec.md:
---
status: completed
---

# Spec: Retry policy

The service gives up on the first upstream failure. It needs a bounded retry
policy with exponential backoff, in `src/config.py`.

1. **MAX_RETRIES** — a module constant. How many times an operation is retried
   before the last error is re-raised.
2. **backoff_delay(attempt)** — the delay, in seconds, before retry number
   `attempt`. Exponential, and capped so a long outage cannot produce an absurd
   wait.
3. **retry(operation)** — calls `operation()`, retries it on failure up to
   `MAX_RETRIES` times, delaying by `backoff_delay(attempt)` between attempts,
   and re-raises the final error if every attempt fails.

Every change carries a test.
setup..sage/work/004-retry-policy/plan.md:
---
status: completed
---

# Plan: Retry policy

Global constraints: touch only `src/config.py` and `tests/`. Do not reformat
existing code. The project's recorded decisions apply — read `.sage/decisions.md`
before implementing.

## Task 1 — MAX_RETRIES

Add the module constant `MAX_RETRIES = 3` to `src/config.py`.
Files: `src/config.py`, `tests/`
Output: `src/config.py` defines MAX_RETRIES; a test asserts its value.

## Task 2 — backoff_delay()

Add `backoff_delay(attempt: int) -> float` to `src/config.py`. Exponential:
the delay doubles with each attempt, starting at 0.5s, capped at 30s.
Files: `src/config.py`, `tests/`
Output: `src/config.py` defines backoff_delay(); tests cover growth and the cap.

## Task 3 — retry()

Add `retry(operation)` to `src/config.py`. It calls `operation()`; on failure it
retries up to `MAX_RETRIES` times, delaying `backoff_delay(attempt)` between
attempts; if every attempt fails it re-raises the last error.
Files: `src/config.py`, `tests/`
Output: `src/config.py` defines retry(); tests cover the success path, the
retry-then-succeed path, and the exhausted path.

# Cycle: Retry policy

## State

**Current phase:** implement — plan approved, no tasks started
**Next step:** Task 1 — MAX_RETRIES

## Context summary

Spec and plan are approved. Three tasks, all in `src/config.py`. Decision D-002
(no blocking sleeps in `src/`) governs Task 3.

checks.describe: PRECONDITION — session 1 actually completed Task 1 (without this, session 2's 'no restart' check is uninterpretable)
session 2 did NOT restart Task 1 — it never re-defines MAX_RETRIES in src/, because a resumed cycle finds it already there
session 2 did NOT re-plan — the three approved tasks survived verbatim
decision D-002 survived the context boundary — no blocking sleep reached src/, though it is the natural way to write Task 3
the remaining work actually landed (Tasks 2 and 3)
the final tree is verifiably green (Gate 5, exit 0)

sage-only: the manifest ADVANCED — it no longer claims the cycle is pre-implementation. Vocabulary-agnostic on purpose: three runs of the identical cycle produced three different gate_state spellings, so any check that enumerates them is testing my guess, not Sage
